coredata.dc:description: Corrosion is serious problem in many industries, and, unfortunately, metal structures in particular are susceptible to the damage it can cause. For this reason, it's important to take the necessary steps to protect against corrosion. Organic chemical compounds, which are significant and naturally occurring molecules, have been shown to be very effective corrosion inhibitors. According to research, chemical compounds that contain oxygen, sulphur, and nitrogen have the strongest inhibitory action. Organic and natural corrosion inhibitors protect metal surfaces by creating protective films. This review focused on the effectiveness of natural corrosion inhibitors and organic compounds in resisting corrosion. The review also examined how some of these compounds halted the corrosion of mild steel in acidic solutions using electrochemical impedance and polarization. Adsorption behaviour and thermodynamic variables were also examined. The study further used SEM micrographs to analyze the surface morphology of mild steel. In this review paper, it gives the types of corrosion, inhibitors, control measures and techniques for corrosion control of mild steel. This will give the inhibition mechanism, inhibition efficiency and effective method of corrosion control. Theoretical studies, Activation parameters adsorption studies, surface morphology was also discussed. Overall, this paper will give the glimpses of corrosion control and research gap in the present scenario. This study aims to encourage a wider use of organic corrosion inhibitors by providing a comprehensive overview of current research on this topic.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Corrosion is serious problem in many industries, and, unfortunately, metal structures in particular are susceptible to the damage it can cause. For this reason, it's important to take the necessary steps to protect against corrosion. Organic chemical compounds, which are significant and naturally occurring molecules, have been shown to be very effective corrosion inhibitors. According to research, chemical compounds that contain oxygen, sulphur, and nitrogen have the strongest inhibitory action. Organic and natural corrosion inhibitors protect metal surfaces by creating protective films. This review focused on the effectiveness of natural corrosion inhibitors and organic compounds in resisting corrosion. The review also examined how some of these compounds halted the corrosion of mild steel in acidic solutions using electrochemical impedance and polarization. Adsorption behaviour and thermodynamic variables were also examined. The study further used SEM micrographs to analyze the surface morphology of mild steel. In this review paper, it gives the types of corrosion, inhibitors, control measures and techniques for corrosion control of mild steel. This will give the inhibition mechanism, inhibition efficiency and effective method of corrosion control. Theoretical studies, Activation parameters adsorption studies, surface morphology was also discussed. Overall, this paper will give the glimpses of corrosion control and research gap in the present scenario. This study aims to encourage a wider use of organic corrosion inhibitors by providing a comprehensive overview of current research on this topic.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
The most widely utilized iron alloy is mild steel. It has stronger mechanical durability. Due to its simple processing and inexpensive production, it has been employed extensively in industries. When steel is subjected to procedures involving acid pickling, industrial washing, acid descaling, and oil wet cleaning, corrosion develops and results in considerable financial losses (Matad et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2003; Yadav et al., 2016). The best and most affordable methods to inhibit corrosion are through the administration of inhibitors. Generally, corrosion inhibitors are with aromatic and heterocyclic rings and functional groups of oxygen, nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulphate (Obot et al., 2015; Varadaraj et al., 2022; Rubaye et al., 2015) (Tables 1 and 2
).

Corrosion inhibitor molecules are absorbed on the metal surface, they create barriers that screen mild steel from the majority of the solution and inhibit corrosion (Gómez et al., 2006; Verma et al., 2007; Kelly et al., 2002). Oxygen, nitrogen and sulphur atoms donate electrons to the metal surface and form chemisorption and sometimes physisorption on the metal surface. Natural inhibitors and synthetic inhibitors are used to reduce the corrosion. Natural inhibitors are popular because of environmental friendliness and economical aspects. Natural corrosion inhibitors, derived from plants or minerals, offer an eco-friendly, sustainable alternative to synthetic inhibitors. They are biodegradable, have low toxicity, and can be expensive. However, they have limitations, such as limited effectiveness in severe corrosion environments. Despite these, natural inhibitors offer sustainability, have potential applications in industries like oil and gas, food, and pharmaceuticals, and are suitable for historical artifact preservation (Elmsellem et al., 2019; Fekkar et al., 2020; Hafez et al., 2019).

In contrast to natural inhibitors, Synthetic inhibitors are exceptionally effective corrosion prevention solutions. Their reliability, longevity, and versatility make them ideal for harsh, corrosive environments, such as offshore platforms, pipelines, and nuclear power plants. Furthermore, they form protective films on metal surfaces to prevent corrosion. This makes them an ideal solution for applications where maintenance is impractical or costly (Attabi et al., 2019; Chkirateet al., 2021; Douche et al., 2020; Elmsellem et al., 2014; Chkirate et al., 2021; Azgaou et al., 2022). Synthetic inhibitors are widely used in industrial applications due to their effectiveness, but their potential environmental impact must be carefully managed to prevent damage and interference with other chemical processes.

In summary, Corrosion inhibitors, whether natural or synthetic, are essential for protecting metal surfaces from corrosion and extending their lifespan. Synthetic inhibitors are more commonly used in industrial applications because of their effectiveness. The type of inhibitor that is most appropriate depends on the specific application, the severity of the corrosion environment, and the desired level of efficacy. Therefore, protecting metal surfaces from corrosion and prolonging their lifespan can be achieved with the aid of both natural and artificial corrosion inhibitors. The detailed submission, the degree of corrosiveness of the surrounding situation, and the wanted level of effectiveness will all influence the sort of inhibitor that is utilized.

Electrochemical measurements like Tafel and Impedance measurements are faster and give accurate results with micro and Nano current range. These findings are supported by adsorption isotherms, activation parameters, and thermodynamic parameters, in addition to the corrosion inhibition experiments. Additionally supporting the inhibition investigations is quantum and molecular dynamics research.

This study aims to provide an overview of general research on different types of inhibitors and their effects on metal corrosion. Take into account the significant developments in the field of corrosion research. An overview of recent research on the inhibitory effect of different natural and synthetic corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in an acidic medium is provided in this article. This article provides a thorough overview of recent research on this topic in an effort to promote the use of organic and natural corrosion inhibitors more widely. Many authors have reported on an array of literature evaluations of corrosion inhibitors, which are based on the inhibitors' application, classification, and inhibitory mechanism. This paper provides a brief summary of all these reviews. There is also discussion of multiple aspects of corrosion inhibitors, including the latest findings and trends.
There exist several kinds of corrosion, comprising (Verma et al., 2017a, b).

Tiny pits or holes that form on a metal surface are a characteristic of this kind of corrosion. It is brought on by localized corrosion, which often results from both external and internal sources such elevated chloride levels.
This is the most prevalent kind of corrosion, spreading uniformly across a material's large surface. It results from exposure to an environment that is corrosive, like water, air, or chemicals.

In this type of corrosion, an electrolyte such as saltwater occurs when two distinct metals come into contact with one another. While one metal turns anodic and corrodes, the other becomes cathodic and protects it from corrosion.
This type of corrosion develops in small spaces where liquid and air flow is restricted, like seams or cracks. This causes corrosive materials to accumulate, causing the material to deteriorate.
This type of corrosion causes material loss at the material's grain boundaries, which weakens the structure. It is often caused by impurities such as chlorine or sulphur.

This specific kind of corrosion happens when a material erodes away from exposure to a highly corrosive fluid traveling at a fast speed. Pipelines, valves, and pumps are commonly affected by this type of corrosion.
When a substance is under stress and in an environment that is corrosive, it can corrode. Elevated temperatures, increased chloride levels, or high tensile tension are frequently the causes.

In summary, the type of corrosion that a material experiences, as seen in Fig. 1 , is dependent upon multiple factors, such as the material composition, the corrosive surroundings, and the exposure circumstances. To effectively prevent and mitigate material corrosion, it is imperative to have a thorough understanding of these aspects.

Chemicals are included as corrosion inhibitors to prevent corrosive conditions in relatively small concentrations or delay corrosion without significantly interacting with the surrounding elements (Verma et al., 2017a, b). From 1 to 15,000 ppm, the concentration can change (Rathod et al., 2022). Corrosion inhibitors are essential in industries such as chemical, oil extraction, and processing. In the presence of corrosion inhibitors, the corrosion process is either delayed or the rate of metal oxidation is slowed (Chitra et al., 2010) By coating a whole metallic substrate with inhibitors, the technique of inhibition stops corrosive species from attacking it (Bentiss et al., 1999).

There are many definitions for inhibitors; however, according to the International Standard Organization, “an inhibitor is a chemical substance that retards corrosion when introduced into an environment in relatively low concentration without significantly changing the concentration of any other corrosive agent.” The metallic surface is shielded by inhibitors that have been adsorbed there. Inhibitors inhibit the corrosion by reducing the mobility of metal ions through the polarization effect. Inhibitors make metallic surfaces more electrically resistive. Corrosion inhibitors effectively cause ions and molecules to adsorb on metal surfaces and decrease reactant diffusion to the metal surface. Both chemical and inorganic inhibitors can be categorized as inhibitors. The activities of inorganic inhibitors might be either cathodic or anodic.

The organic inhibitors can operate either as an adsorbent or as a cathodic-anodic combination. Inorganic corrosion inhibitors function better over a wider range of temperatures for longer periods of time than organic corrosion inhibitors do. Organic corrosion inhibitors are less damaging than inorganic corrosion inhibitors, although they are more expensive. Organic inhibitors have the capability to adsorb on metal surfaces by utilizing heteroatoms like Oxygen, Nitrogen, Sulphur, and Phosphorous (Sanyal, 1981; Verma et al., 2021).

Amino acids, alcohols, and amines are examples of organic molecules found in these inhibitors. In order to shield the metal from the corrosive atmosphere, they adhere to the surface of the metal. Petroleum, oil and gas, and marine applications are among the industries that frequently use organic inhibitors.

Utilizing organic substances as inhibitors is one of the best techniques for preventing metals corrosion, particularly in conditions of acidity. Many organic inhibitors have been investigated for their corrosion inhibition abilities. Organic compounds with atoms of sulphur, nitrogen, and oxygen corrode less quickly because molecules that stop corrosion are adsorbed on the metal's surface.

The absorption of corrosion inhibitors is mainly governed by the physical characteristics of a molecule, including its functional group, molecular weight, steric factor, structure, aromaticity, electron density of the donor atoms, and p-orbital nature of the donating electrons. The electrical structure of the molecules also matters a lot.

The substances that are utilized as organic inhibitors can function either as cathodic, anodic, or cathodic-anodic, either separately or in combination. Using the surface adsorption method, they can also create a film. Some examples are benzotriazole, benzthiazole, mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT), amines, urea, heterocyclic nitrogen, and sulphur-containing substances, as well as caffeine, succinic acid, ascorbic acid, and extracts of numerous natural resources. Ethanolamine carbonate, dicycloexilamonio benzoate, and diisopropylammonium nitrite are examples of corrosion inhibitors for the volatile vapor (Goyal et al., 2018; Stupnišek-Lisac et al., 1994; Schmitt and Bedbur, 1985).
There are various categories of organic inhibitors Fig. 2 .
These inhibitors are often found in boilers and cooling water systems, where their active ingredient is nitrite ions.
This kind of inhibitor effectively stops corrosion in systems with high temperatures and high pressures because it includes derivatives of phosphonic acid.
Benzoic acid, acetic acid and salicylic acid are examples of chemicals that are often utilized as carboxylic acid inhibitors in industrial settings.
Sulfonic acid inhibitors, which are mixtures of sulfonic acid and its derivatives, are used in many industrial applications.
High-temperature systems can effectively be kept corrosion-free by using thiophosphoric acid inhibitors, which are composed of thiophosphoric acid and its derivatives.
This class of inhibitor is employed in numerous industrial applications and has ethylenediamine as its active component.
Amine inhibitors are frequently employed in oilfield and water treatment applications, where their active component is an amine molecule.
Phenol inhibitors are utilized in many industrial applications and use phenol or its derivatives as the active component.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional groups in organic corrosion inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: The proficiency of organic corrosion inhibitors is largely dependent on active functional groups. In order to prevent further corrosion, these functional groups form a protective layer by interacting with the metal surface. Some of the active functional groups found in organic corrosion inhibitors that are most commonly used are listed below (Devendra et al., 2022a,b; Talat et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2019).

Those functional groups contain nitrogen atoms, which combine with metal ions to form chelating agents. Guanidines, imides, and amines are some of the most common nitrogen-containing functional groups utilized in corrosion inhibitors.

The oxygen atoms in these functional groups can form a protective layer on the metal surface to inhibit corrosion. Carboxylic acids, esters, and ethers are most frequently utilized as corrosion inhibitors.

Organic compounds that contain sulphur possess the capacity to coat the metal's surface with a protective film, halting further corrosion. Sulfonic acids and thiols are most frequently utilized in corrosion inhibitors.

Functional groups containing phosphorus have the ability to create a protective layer that stops additional corrosion on the metal surface. Phosphates and phosphonic acids represent the predominant functional groups containing phosphorus found in corrosion prevention.
Halogens, such as Cl, F, and Br, can coat metal surfaces to prevent further corrosion.

In conclusion, the usefulness of organic corrosion prevention depends on functional clusters. These active functional groups can stop further corrosion by creating a protective layer on the metal surface, which increases the lifespan of metal components.

We must examine the corrosion mechanism, the factors that contribute to metallic corrosion, the behaviour of metals in various media (such as water, the environment, and the existence of various gases and salts), and, of course, the various corrosion conditions (including cavitation, pitting, erosion, galvanic, MIC, and others). In order to choose the appropriate corrosion control measures. Identifying possible issues with corrosion and designing appropriate anti-corrosion strategies in response to them are critical. To prevent or minimize the probability of galvanic corrosion, for instance, it is crucial to choose alloys with close electrode potentials, electrically isolate different alloys, or increase an anodic area in the direction of a cathodic area when designing new equipment. If a liquid's flow velocity is the primary cause of corrosion, we must either choose an appropriate alloy or protective layer that is repellent to erosion or cavitation, or we must take action to adjust the velocity (increasing in the particular case of decreased velocity and stagnation or lowering in the case of turbulence). In the event that using a corrosion-resistant alloy or appropriate protective coating is not feasible, we can utilize cathodic protection, a corrosion inhibitor, or a temperature above the dew point. We can categorize the corrosion prevention techniques into six categories.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The use of metallic, inorganic, and organic coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the composition of the basic material, all coatings can be classified into three categories: organic, metallic, and inorganic. Paints, polymer compounds, oils, and other paraffinic mixes can all be considered organic coatings. Glasses, cement-based ceramics, and enamels are examples of inorganic coatings. Metals or alloys can be coated with metallic materials. Let's start with the organic coatings. When pigments (oxides, phosphates, and other salts) are added to paints, their galvanic action, along with the physical barrier against aggressive substances, provide the overall protective mechanism of organic coatings. The porosity of coatings is typically reduced by using many layers. Sometimes the term “primer” refers to the first layer of coating. Surface of the metal and the primer molecules react when the primer contains phosphoric acid, forming chemical bonds between the atoms on the metal surface and the primer molecules. Polar group-containing compounds, such as imines (-NH), carboxyls (-COOH), and hydroxyls (-OH), serve as the foundation for a large number of organic coating materials. When paint comes into physical contact with a coating, it sticks to the surface. Coatings will adhere to metal surfaces less successfully if they contain any impurities like oil, salts, grease, dust, dirt, water, and old paint. Therefore, adhesion depends on surface preparation (Groysman and Groysman, 2010; Yang et al., 2021; Kavirajwar et al., 2022; Ballinger and Lim, 2004; Devendra et al., 2021a,b; Groysman and Groysman, 2010; Baraka et al., 2003; El Faydy et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The use of anodic and cathodic protection electrochemical techniques
ce:section.ce:para: The corrosion process through electrochemistry in electrolytes makes it possible to stop metal from corroding by applying electric current and electric potential. Thus, electrochemical techniques are limited to electrolyte solutions. Anodic and cathodic protection is collectively referred to as electrochemical techniques. Since they are distinct, mixing them is not advisable. When metallic components are connected to the power source's negative pole, they entirely transform into a cathode that is resistant to corrosion, providing cathodic protection. Passivity is the basis of anodic protection. In order to achieve the passive condition of anodic protection, metallic components that are linked to the positive pole of the power supply experience a positive change in electric potential. Therefore, anodic protection against corrosion is applicable to metals and alloys that can exist in a passive state in a specific kind of medium (Jiang et al., 2018; Chaouiki et al., 2019; Verma et al., 2016; Sayyid et al., 2022).

Applying anti-corrosion agents; eliminating aggressive elements like ammonia, hydrogen sulphide, chlorides, and oxygen, neutralization (adding acids or alkalis to solutions that are acidic or alkali); the elimination of water vapor from the atmosphere, drying it; using biocides etc. (Ouakki et al., 2023; El Ashry et al., 2012; Ouakki et al., 2021).

Composites, polymeric materials, ceramics, glasses, and metals and alloys resistant to corrosion. Remember, there isn't a metal or other substance that works for every situation or in every medium. High temperatures and mechanical strains are not things that polymeric materials can withstand. Glasses and ceramic materials break easily. High temperatures, certain chemicals, and their relative cost are among the things that composite materials cannot withstand (Liu et al., 2021; Rezaeivala et al., 2022).

To prevent galvanic corrosion, structures of the metal as well as equipment must be constructed with ease of cleaning, surface preparation, painting, and avoiding sharp elbows or dissimilar alloys in an electrolyte solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Technological interventions, such as altering the parameters of the process
ce:section.ce:para: As an instance, maintaining a temperature of 20–30oC above the dewpoint inhibits the condensation of corrosive substances like H2O with dissolved HCl or H2SO4, reducing the liquid's flow velocity during erosion or increasing it during stagnation to prevent the formation of deposits such as fouling and, consequently, a potential under-deposit corrosion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combining techniques to prevent corrosion are also known
ce:section.ce:para: To prevent corrosion, we can add inhibitors to paints, concrete, cathodic protection in conjunction with protective coatings and corrosion inhibitors, maintain a temperature above dewpoint, and inject inhibitors and neutralizers into streams of processing. It is evident that a wide variety of corrosion control methods exist. To help us choose the right one and keep metallic buildings from corroding, we will get to know a few of them in the parts that follow (Saranya et al., 2020; Carranza et al., 2021; Dawood et al., 2021; Hosseini et al., 2017; Ali et al., 2005; Rathod et al., 2021).

Corrosion inhibitors function by protecting the metal surface with a protective coating, inhibiting the development of corrosion materials at the end, and shielding the metal after deterioration. But in order to make sure these inhibitors are functioning as planned and to find out if the system needs to be adjusted, it's critical to calculate their (Lowmunkhong et al., 2010) Several methodologies exist for determining the efficacy of corrosion inhibitors, either synthetic or natural.

The metal samples were weighed both before and after they were submerged in acidic solution in the presence and absence of various concentrations of inhibitors enabled us to compute the weight loss of the precleaned and dried mild steel specimens. Weight loss experiments were conducted, and the average result is shown. The connection was used to calculate mild steel's corrosion rate (νcorr).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of corrosion inhibitors on the rate and efficiency of corrosion inhibition
ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of the organic inhibitors to inhibit corrosion can be evaluated by implementing weight loss method. Because of its simplicity and reliability, this corrosion rate monitoring approach is advantageous. The parameters of corrosion obtained from the weight loss method of mild steel in an acidic solution at various temperatures and in the presence and absence of various concentrations of corrosion inhibitors show that the ηw increases and vcorr values decline with inhibitor concentration. This is because the inhibitor's concentration increases the amount of the inhibitor that adsorbs on the mild steel surface (Mahdi et al., 2022). The effect of temperature on corrosion inhibitors' behaviour in terms of corrosion inhibition was investigated at 303–333 K. Corrosion rate accelerates, and corrosion inhibitors' ability to prevent corrosion weakens with rising temperatures.

The temperature-dependent decrease in ηw is produced by corrosion inhibitors at lower concentrations. As the temperature-dependent drop in IE suggests, physical adsorption is the most significant factor (Nishikata et al., 1995) Mild steel dissolves more quickly in an acidic media due to the normal acceleration of hydrogen gas evolution brought on by temperature increases. As a result, a rise in temperature accelerated mild steel's surface dissolution and decreased the quantity of inhibitor adsorption. Nevertheless, when corrosion inhibitor concentrations are higher, the impact of temperature increases on ηw is negligible.

The consistency of the efficiency of inhibitors at increased concentrations within the different temperatures under investigation can be attributed to a little variation in the adsorption method, wherein chemisorption unaccompanied or associated with physisorption is possible (Amiery et al., 2023).

Potentiodynamic polarization and EIS are useful methods for determining the effectiveness of organic inhibitors. These approaches provide insights into corrosion rates and assess voltage and current changes caused by the presence of inhibitors (Desai et al., 2023).

Potentiodynamic polarization provides an efficient means of assessing the extent to which organic inhibitors mitigate metal corrosion (Desai et al., 2023) Using this method, a regulated voltage is applied to a metal surface that is immersed in a solution having a reference electrode and an inhibitor. By monitoring the current responses over a period of time and evaluating the information, one can ascertain the current and corrosion potential. Variations in potential & current indicate how effectively the inhibitor slows down corrosion. The outcome of this analysis can help increase inhibitor concentrations and corrosion inhibition conditions. Corrosion inhibitor can decrease the corrosion rate by delaying the anodic & cathodic processes (Devendra et al., 2021a,b; Jeeva et al., 2017; Behpour et al., 2008; Keleş et al., 2021).

Anodic reactions oxidize metals, whereas cathodic reactions deplete oxidants. Inhibitors can reduce the rate of cathodic reactions by lowering the driving force for the reduction reaction, competing with the oxidant for electrons, or obstructing the reduction site. They can also reduce the rate of anodic reactions by blocking anodic sites, increasing the activation energy of the oxidation process, or forming a passive layer on the surface of the metal that inhibits metal disintegration (Winkler et al., 2014).

By forming a passive coating on the surface of the metal and keeping it from dissolving, anodic inhibitors reduce the rate of anodic reactions. Through adsorption on the metal surface and protective film formation, cathodic inhibitors reduce the rate of cathodic reactions by preventing electrons from interacting with the oxidant. Amines, nitrates, and nitrites are a few types of cathodic inhibitors. By interacting with the oxidant for electrons and creating a protective film on the metal's surface, mixed-type inhibitors can suppress both the cathodic and anodic processes. Benzotriazole, tolyltriazole, and mercaptobenzothiazole are a few examples of mixed-type inhibitors (Toukal et al., 2022a, b).

In conclusion, inhibitors are chemicals that operate on the anodic and cathodic processes to slow down the corrosion's rate. Based on how they work, these inhibitors can be classified as anodic, cathodic, and mixed type. The metal's composition and the corrosion-prone environment it is exposed to determine which inhibitor is best. Potentiodynamic polarization predicts the current being function of potential when a potential ramp is applied to the surface of the metal. The estimates can then be employed to determine the corrosion potential, current, and inhibitor efficiency.

A solution containing inhibitors must be added to the metal before initiating an experiment on potentiodynamic polarization. An electronic potentiostat is used to apply a potential ramp to the metal and measure the current that comes out. One tool for figuring out a metal's potential for corrosion is a potentiostat. This is achieved by measuring the current that arises from providing a potential ramp for the metal. Typically, the gradient starts at a potential greater than the corrosion potential (cathodic), passes through the zero prospective, and ends at a prospective higher than the corrosion prospective (anodic). The particular metal and the characteristics of its corrosion determine the exact potential range that is employed. In order to ensure a complete reduction of the metal prior to measurement, the potential at which the ramp starts could be lower than the corrosion potential. The corrosion potential is determined by measuring the potential at which corrosion occurs when the ramp goes past it. When the potential exceeds the corrosion potential, the ramp is stopped to assure complete oxidation of the metal.

When the positive potential exceeds the corrosion potential, it usually means that the positive potential is increasing. Nevertheless, depending on the angle of the ramp, potential values might be both positive and negative (Amiery et al., 2023) The corrosion current and potential can be calculated using the following formulas based on the experimentally obtained current potential curve.

The corrosion current and potential recorded in its presence and absence can be compared to determine the presence of the inhibitor. The inhibitor's ability to prevent corrosion is demonstrated by a decrease in both the corrosion potential and current when it is present. Because of the nature of the inhibitor and the particulars of the study, they are simply estimates that may need to be modified.

EIS is a beneficial and advantageous technique for figuring out metal impedance. A small sinusoidal voltage is applied to the surface of the metal to assess the current response and ascertain the impedance of the metal inhibitor system. EIS, which uses sophisticated impedance measurements, provides a thorough understanding of the corrosion mechanism, including the effects of various inhibitors, corrosion rates, root causes, and inhibitor-metal surface interactions. This approach is useful in a variety of settings, including wet and corrosive situations. It can also be used to track the efficacy of inhibitors and recognize potential imperfections in protective coatings (A. Jawad et al., 2019) Material scientists, corrosion scientists, and industrial scientists can all benefit from the use of EIS, which is frequently operating within the research of inhibitors. Additionally, it is used in the development of coatings and materials that are resistant to corrosion. Consequently, EIS is an effective and adaptable technique for researching the prevention of corrosion in metal systems (Toukal et al., 2022a, b).

The complex quantity known as the impedance can be represented by the equation below:
Z = R + jX
where R = resistance and X = reactance.

The following equations can be employed to convert the impedance into a phase angle (θ) and magnitude (Z′):

The impedance spectrum can be obtained by plotting the impedance's magnitude and phase angle against frequency. An organic inhibitor will cause the impedance spectrum to change, which can be used to measure the metal's potential and corrosion current. The corrosion current can be computed by applying the impedance values to a Randle's equivalent circuit, which consists of a series combination of an electrode resistance, a constant phase element, and a Warburg's impedance.

The corrosion potential is calculated from the impedance spectrum by determining the frequency at which the impedance magnitude reaches its minimum. This frequency represents the organic inhibitor's greatest penetration into the metal surface and corresponds closely with the corrosion potential. The corrosion potential can be calculated using this equation.
E corr

E 0

)
where,

E
0 = Standard potential of the metal, k = Boltzmann constant, T = temperature, q = electron charge, f = frequency, and f
o = constant.

In a nutshell EIS and potentiodynamic polarization should be considered when estimating the corrosion potential and current in metals containing organic inhibitors. They provide information on activation energy, diffusion coefficient, and inhibition efficiency in order to optimize the effectiveness of organic inhibitors in preventing corrosion.

Using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), an advanced imaging technology that can provide high-quality pictures of metal surfaces, one can conduct a thorough investigation of the corrosion, protective film, and underlying surfaces of the metals (Toukal et al., 2022a, b). In this method, an electron beam is directed towards a sample, and through surface interaction, secondary electrons are produced. These electrons are then gathered to generate a high-quality picture(Bokati and Dehghanian, 2018).One advantage of scanning electron microscopy is its adaptability, which enables it to be used in environmental chambers, high- and low-vacuum settings, and to examine corrosion processes in various situations (Lopez et al., 2003) SEM can be used in combination with different methods, such as X-ray spectroscopy, for the chemical assessment of corrosion products in order to regulate the kind of corrosion and where it originates. SEM is a vital instrument to investigate corrosion and assessing preventative actions in general. It provides useful information about inhibitor to enhance defence strategies and lengthen the life of metal structures. One study examined the effectiveness of a recently created organic corrosion inhibitor using SEM testing.

The surface of a metal sample can be imaged in high resolution using SEM, a powerful technology that makes it possible to characterize the topography, morphology, and composition of the sample. A direct estimate of metal potential and the corrosion current in the existence of an organic corrosion inhibitor is not usually done using SEM. The corrosion potential and current are usually measured electrochemically, using methods like potentiodynamic polarization or EIS.

With these methods, a small DC or AC voltages is applied to the metal, and the potential responses and the current resulting from it are estimated. Utilizing the information gathered from these tests, one may assess how well an inhibitor is working to prevent corrosion as well as describe how the metal behaves when corrosion is present or absent (Bruemmer and Thomas, 2001; Volovitch et al., 2009; Devendra and Praveen, 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The following steps are dealt with in the technique
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of the sample: Prior to SEM analysis, the metal sample is cleaned and free of any surface impurities. This is usually done with an etching or solvent cleaning procedure.

Organic inhibitor deposition: This is used to coat the surface of the metal. Several techniques, including chemical deposition, electrode position, and physical adsorption, can be used to accomplish this.

Scanning Electrode Microscopy (SEM) imaging: Next, The SEM is used to examine the material and Imaging is carried out. An electron beam with high energy is used by scanning electron microscopy in order to scan the material and produce a high-resolution image. This image can be used for assessing the organic inhibitor's distribution on the metal surface.

Current-potential estimation: Current potential is estimated using the potential difference between the metal sample and a reference electrode. The metal ions are then pushed toward the reference electrode by the electric field created as a result of this. The corrosion current, calculated using equations rather than actual measurement, defines the rate of ion movement.

Data Analysis: Using appropriate equations, the potential and corrosion current density can be computed using the estimated current-potential. The corrosion potential is ascertainable through the utilization of the Nernst equation, or Ecorr:
E
c o r r
=
E 0
‐
(
R T / l n F
)
ln
(
i /
i
c o r r
)
where n = number of electrons transported during the process, F = Faraday constant, i = estimated current, icorr = corrosion current, R = constant, T = temperature, and Eo = standard potential.

Results interpretation: One can assess the effectiveness of the organic inhibitor by looking at the corrosion potential and current. The organic inhibitor is effectively stopping corrosion, as evidenced by a reduced corrosion current and a larger corrosion potential.

Metal sample surface shape and composition can be examined using SEM, but the prospective or current rate of corrosion cannot be determined with this technique. Measurements of polarization using potentiostatic or potentiodynamic methods are used (Deyá and Carrizo, 2022).

SEM can, nevertheless, continue to be a valuable tool for understanding a material's corrosion behaviour. It offers details on the many forms of corrosion products and their distribution throughout the corrosion mechanism, as well as the topography and morphology of the corroded metal surface. Together with electrochemical tests, this data can be utilized to obtain a more thorough an image depicting the corrosion behaviour of the material.

SEM, for instance, can be used to look at a corroded metal sample's surface both before to and following electrochemical analyses are made. Scholars can get additional information regarding how the corrosion mechanism (process) has changed the surface of the metal and by comparing photographs taken before and after the electrochemical experiments, one can determine how the surface is related to the electrochemical parameters.

Despite its inability to directly detect corrosion current and potential, SEM provides valuable insights on the types and distribution of corrosion products, as well as the morphology and topography of damaged surfaces. This information, when paired with electrochemical studies, can provide a far better insight of a material's corrosion properties (Amiery et al., 2023). Evaluating the efficacy of organic corrosion inhibitors is critical for guaranteeing metal systems' longevity and integrity. Various approaches can be employed to determine these inhibitors' efficiency, and it's crucial to choose the right ones based on the system's particular (Chauhan et al., 2021).

EDX is a chemical analysis technique that uses an energetic electron beam to focus on samples, excitation electrons from inner shells, and simultaneous emission of X-ray photons. The energy content of these photons is specific to the element they are emitted from, allowing for the analysis of the sample's chemical composition.

SEM-EDX is a high-magnification, stereoscopic microscopy method used to analyze healing products. It uses a focused high-energy electron beam to scan samples, eliciting physical information about the sample's surface topography. This method is commonly used to identify crystal structure, morphology, size, and distribution. The composition of the interface is measured in backscattered SEM images. Inorganic healing agents often contain Ca, Si, Al, Mg hydrates, and carbonates.

SEM/EDX is a technique that provides a pictorial representation of a surface with the elemental composition of chosen areas. It is sensitive to elements up to 0.1 wt % and can probe depths from 0.2 to 8 μm. This technique is often used for initial analysis with unknown samples. SEM/EDX does not provide oxidation state information like XPS, but it provides spatial resolution of the surface down to the nanometre range. It is also used to study cross sections of material, determining which layer in a multilayer material causes defects. SEM and optical microscopy are often complementary techniques (Scimeca et al., 2014a,b; Scimeca et al., 2017; Barba et al., 2015; Khan et al., 2015; Scimeca et al., 2014a,b).

Since corrosion is predominantly a surface phenomenon, its effects on an asset's performance, durability, and reliability are significant. In fact, the costs of repairs and refurbishment connected to corrosion can reach the millions of dollars. It is worth noting that direct corrosion costs in the Middle East are projected to be around 5% of GDP, which is a significant financial burden. In this regard, one of the primary reasons for equipment and component failure in a variety of industrial industries is metal corrosion. The bulk of component failures in the oil and gas industry are caused by severe operating conditions and corrosive surroundings, which also increase the amount and severity of corrosion. Thus, for effective and economical asset integrity management, a thorough understanding of the corrosion mechanisms and processes occurring on a component's surface is essential. Numerous sophisticated analytical methods offer valuable quantitative data on corrosion, including X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray fluorescence (XRF) spectroscopy, localized electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (LEIS), scanning vibrating electrode technique (SVET), and scanning Ion-Selective Electrode Technique (SIET).

One of the most advanced methods among them is X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which may be used to investigate damaged surfaces, protective coatings, and different corrosion mechanisms. Additionally, XPS is a useful tool for evaluating corrosion byproducts in order to comprehend the varied surface reactions, localized surface chemistry, and changes in electrical responses in corrosive settings. This chapter is devoted to the application of XPS technology in protective coatings for materials. A particular focus is on its involvement in compositional analysis of different kinds of coatings (metallic and polymeric) that are being researched in the field of corrosion protection. In addition, smart polymeric coatings that verify the self-release of different inhibitors and self-healing agents contained in nanocarriers can be explored using XPS.

Additionally, the function of XPS in post-corrosion investigation is explained, offering sufficient details to comprehend the corrosion prevention mechanism. For the readers' interest and desire to succeed in this crucial field of research, a brief remark on the potential applications and difficulties of XPS technology in corrosion research is also included.

Adhesion Issues with XPS When examining a failing surface from both sides as well as the component cross section, a plethora of information can be discovered. The manufacturer and the analyst can identify the layer in which the bond has failed since the compositions of the two sides of a failed bond are typically comparable. A failure in the metal/metal oxide layer may have occurred if the bond was between a metal and polymer and there was metal on both sides of the broken bond.

XPS analysis can assist in identifying which of the two interfaces has failed if a third component such as an adhesive is present in the sample. XPS is highly helpful in laminate analysis for these reasons. Using PVC-based glue, the producer attempted to join a moulded piece of polyvinyl chloride (PVC) to another PVC component. Though one specific set of moulded components was not adhering adequately, the method had previously worked effectively.

While it is frequently challenging to pinpoint the location of a failure when the composition of all the materials is similar, XPS analysis proved to be effective in this particular example. Additives are commonly found in PVC, with organo-tin additives being one of the many. Where the moulded PVC surface meets (Baer et al., 2019; Linford et al., 2020; Engelhard et al., 2020; Morgan, 2023; Shard, 2020).

This phenomenon called “Dynamic Recrystallization” (DRX) happens when you strain metals at higher temperatures. This is characterised by a nucleation rate of low disruption density grains and a rear growth rate that, upon reaching equilibrium, it may result in consistent grain size. DRX, also known as discontinuous dynamic recrystallization, happens beyond a certain strain threshold, εc. The type of strain path, the initial grain size, D0, temperature, T, and strain rate, ε, all determine this threshold. Once the critical strain is reached, in face cantered cubic (fcc) metals with medium to low stacking fault energy, the primary mechanisms responsible for the stress-strain response shift from strain-hardening and dynamic recovery to dynamic recrystallization (DRX). DRX is not limited to fcc metals and has been observed in ice, certain minerals, and even high purity α–Fe. This transition of softening mechanism from strain-hardening and dynamic recovery to DRX is the reason why it is referred to as a discontinuous process (Yamasaki et al., 2011).

When healing and strain hardening are unable to hold any more immobile dislocations, DRX starts at the microstructural level. Up until a new grain forms, the grain becomes saturated with dislocation barriers in the form of cells, causing the grain borders to bulge. One can say that the hot flow curve exhibits monotonic stress behaviour if the equilibrium or stable condition is attained in a single cycle. The stress decreases at a specific kinetic rate after reaching the peak stress, σp, and reaches the steady state stress, σss. Stress behaviour is referred to as multiple peak type if oscillations occur before the steady state is reached, leading to many cycles of recrystallization and grain development. This unique stress behaviour prior to reaching the steady state is dependent upon the temperature, strain rate, and initial particle size Gottstein et al. (2022); Gourdet and Montheillet, 2003).

This work develops a stress-strain model that reflects both recrystallization approaches and analyses previous theories that predict either numerous peak stress behaviours or monotonic stress behaviour. There are a number of criteria that can be used to predict a monotonic or multiple peak stress behaviour; some of these criteria are specific to materials and test conditions, but more recent and comprehensive theories recognize the relationships between temperature, strain rate, initial grain size, and the stable dynamically. On a true stress-true strain heat flow curve, the onset of DRX was indicated by a stress peak; however, the associated peak strain (εp) does not always match the microstructural critical strain (εcr) (Yamasaki et al., 2022).

In copper, εcr happens far earlier than εp at higher strain rates and lower temperatures, whereas εp and εcr are almost equal at slower strain rates and higher temperatures. The ratio εcr/εp does not change, according to more recent studies on steels, hence the preceding findings remain valid. In the subsection on the beginning of DRX, this seeming conflict will be clarified. A thorough analysis of both strain-related concepts is necessary to comprehend the difficulties associated with microstructural prediction, which is the aim of any extended model. The microstructural alteration brought on by DRX is thought to have halted when the rate of nucleation and development of the grains reaches equilibrium. At this point, the initial grains are either eaten or have been replaced by older, more dislocation-free grains. The mechanical characteristics of DRX improve at room temperature, making it of industrial importance when the new homogenous dynamically recrystallized grains are smaller than the original grains. Stress is largely consistent despite on-going strain following the microstructural alteration brought on by DRX (Nie et al., 2021).

Hot deformation results in simultaneous work hardening and dynamic softening, affecting the mechanical feedback and workability of alloys. Dynamic recrystallization (DRX) is crucial for refining the microstructure of metallic alloys and achieving a stable equilibrium state during hot deformation. This process leads to the formation of new equiaxed recrystallized grains, enhancing mechanical properties. Achieving a homogeneous distribution of β grains within the single phase region is of particular interest. Traditional DRX has been extensively studied, but fine and uniform DRX has received limited research (Zhang et al., 2021).

Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) works by using a probe to scan across the surface and measure surface topography. A probe is rastered across the surface and is deflected as it encounters features on the surface. The technique generates a topographical map from which average roughness data, line scans, and histograms can be obtained. Additionally, AFM gathers information on surface properties such as adhesion, friction, and mechanical properties (e.g., stiffness and elasticity) through its measurements. This versatility makes AFM a valuable tool for analysing a wide range of materials and surfaces, including polymers, ceramics, and biological samples. AFM can provide high-resolution images with very precise measurements at the nanometre level, enabling detailed analysis of surface structures and properties crucial for advancements in materials science and biological research. Furthermore, AFM assesses the mechanical behaviour of surfaces by examining parameters such as adhesion forces and stiffness (Knapek et al., 2018; El Aziz Fouda and Ali, 2018).

The data obtained from the AFM analysis revealed intricate details of the surface topography, such as specific roughness patterns, grain boundary structures, and distinct step heights. Through meticulous analysis of the AFM images and data, we gained valuable insights into the material properties, such as understanding the crystallographic orientation, and surface morphology at the nanoscale level. Furthermore, the AFM measurements enabled us to precisely quantify parameters such as surface roughness and peak-to-valley height, crucial for understanding the material's performance and behaviour in diverse applications. In conclusion, the utilization of AFM emerged as a potent tool for comprehensive characterization and study of the sample's surface properties, offering detailed insights unattainable through conventional methods (Setvín et al., 2017).

The 3-dimensional photographs and elevation pattern of the shiny mild steel surface, without and with inhibitor, for a 2 h immersion time in 1 M hydrochloric acid.The average roughness (Ra) of the shiny mild steel surface is 0.5 μm, in contrast to the 1.2 μm roughness observed on the corroded surface. Following exposure to 1 M hydrochloric acid for 2 h, the mild steel surface shows a significant increase in roughness to 1.8 μm, attributed to cracking and surface degradation induced by the acidic conditions. In the presence of the inhibitor, a protective film forms on the surface of the mild steel, and the Ra value closely matches that of the shiny steel, indicating successful inhibitor adsorption and effective protection of the metal from corrosion. The favourable results of AFM are crucial as they provide valuable surface information that complements and enhances the SEM studies, offering a comprehensive understanding of the steel surface characteristics under investigation (Singh et al., 2020).

In many industrial contexts, organic inhibitors are used to control metal corrosion. These inhibitors are classified as synthetic or natural. Synthetic inhibitors are man-made chemical substances. Natural inhibitors, on the other hand, come from plants and animals. Numerous studies have proved the efficiency of both types of inhibitors in preventing corrosion, and they have been the focus of much research throughout the years (Esmaily et al., 2015).

The use of plant and animal extracts to prevent corrosion has been the primary focus of natural inhibitor research. For instance, research has shown that extracts from many kinds of herbs and spices, including clove, cinnamon, and basil, are useful in halting the corrosive effects of different metals, including copper, aluminium, and iron. The ability of natural inhibitors to form protective layers that prevent corrosion on metal surfaces has been recognized as their mode of action. Additionally, it has been demonstrated that the antioxidants found in natural inhibitors can mitigate the harmful impact that corrosive chemicals have on metals (Zhao et al., 2011).

The primary goal of research on synthetic inhibitors has been to design and synthesize new chemicals with strong corrosion resistance. Because synthetic inhibitors are more soluble in corrosive environments than natural inhibitors, they are superior at controlling corrosion. However, there are concerns regarding the widespread usage of synthetic inhibitors due to the risks they pose to human health and the environment (Fouda et al., 2014). These are a few instances of earlier research on the use of organic inhibitors, both synthetic and natural.

ce:list-item.ce:label: a)
ce:list-item.ce:para: As a natural organic inhibitor, chitosan can be used to stop mild steel from corroding in saltwater (Khan et al., 2022).

ce:list-item.ce:label: b)
ce:list-item.ce:para: The application of an extract from cinnamon to prevent aluminum from corroding in acidic environments (El Mouaden et al., 2018).

ce:list-item.ce:label: c)
ce:list-item.ce:para: Carbon steel is treated with an aqueous solution of tannic acid, a naturally occurring corrosion inhibitor (Bouraoui et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:para: Recent years have seen a notable increase in interest in natural corrosion inhibitors because of their sustainable, affordable, and environmentally beneficial qualities. They come from a variety of natural sources, including microbes, plants, and animals, and research has shown that they offer superior corrosion-inhibition qualities in a range of settings. Several studies have been undertaken to investigate the efficacy of natural organic corrosion inhibitors across diverse industries (Ostovari et al., 2009).

Piper nigrum extract was examined for its efficiency as a natural corrosion inhibitor in a 3.5% NaCl solution using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and potentiodynamic polarization techniques (Umarani and Ngobiri, 2021). In comparison to few commercial inhibitors employed for the same purpose, they discovered that the extract had a substantial inhibitory efficiency. Following electrochemical techniques and weight loss, the efficacy of a polysaccharide obtained from the marine algae Padina tetrastromatica to prevent corrosion in a 3.5% NaCl solution. They found that the polysaccharide was more potent at higher concentrations and exhibited superior corrosion-inhibition properties (Okoronkwo et al., 2015).

Additionally, a few investigations have looked into the complementary impact of additional chemicals and natural corrosion inhibitors. In comparison to the inhibitors used alone, mixing the extracts with sodium molybdate resulted in higher inhibitory efficiency (Devendra et al., 2022a,b).

ce:list-item.ce:label: a)
ce:list-item.ce:para: Efficacy of Benzotriazole in preventing aluminum alloy corrosion in acidic conditions (Bokati and Dehghanian, 2018)

ce:list-item.ce:label: b)
ce:list-item.ce:para: The application of triazole derivatives in acidic settings to suppress mild steel corrosion (Resende et al., 2019).

ce:list-item.ce:label: c)
ce:list-item.ce:para: Imidazoline derivatives' ability to suppress copper corrosion in seawater(aerated) (Zarrouk et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, research on both natural and synthetic organic inhibitors has given useful insights into corrosion process and the efficacy of inhibitors in prevnting them. Natural inhibitors are thought to be safer and eco-friendly than synthetic inhibitors, despite the fact that studies have shown them to be less effective (Arrousse et al., 2022).

A separate study looked at the effects of synthetic inhibitors such as mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT), tolyltriazole (TTA), and benzotriazole (BTA) on carbon steel in an acidic environment. Tannic acid, a naturally occurring inhibitor, was also evaluated. The results showed that MBT had the lowest efficacy of the inhibitors examined, whereas tannic acid had the maximum effectiveness, followed by TTA and BTA (Lazrak et al., 2022).

In a third study, the effectiveness of 2-mercaptobenzimidazole (MBI), a synthetic inhibitor, and anillin, a natural inhibitor, in preventing MS corrosion in acidic solutions were contrasted. The observations suggest that vanillin may have been a more effective inhibitor than MBI because it can form a larger protective barrier on the metal surface (Amiery et al., 2023).

Li and colleagues (Ituen et al., 2016) investigated the inhibitory effects of three natural inhibitors (tea, black pepper, and cinnamon) and three synthetic inhibitors (benzotriazole, imidazole, and mercaptobenzimidazole) on the corrosion of carbon steel in acidic solutions. The findings revealed that, while all six inhibitors significantly reduced corrosion, natural inhibitors performed marginally better than synthetic inhibitors.

The reason for this, according to the authors' hypothesis, could be that natural inhibitors include multiple active components that combine to provide a positive effect. In an experiment, El-Etre et al. (Amiery et al., 2023) examined the inhibitory effects of pomegranate peel extract, a natural inhibitor, and sodium benzoate, a synthetic inhibitor, on mild steel corrosion in a 1 M HCl solution. With a corrosion inhibition efficiency of 91.6% as opposed to 87.1%, the natural inhibitor outperformed the synthetic inhibitor in this regard, as the results showed, even though both inhibitors were effective in reducing corrosion. The reason for this, according to the scientists, could be because the natural inhibitor has phenolic compounds and tannins that could create a protective layer on the metal surface.

In contrast, Mekidiche and colleagues (Etre et al., 2003) investigated the inhibitory effects of a natural inhibitor (rosemary essential oil) and a synthetic inhibitor (4-methoxybenzylidene-4-butanoylthiosemicarbazide) on mild steel corrosion in a 1 M HCl solution. The results showed that the synthetic inhibitor outperformed the natural one, with a corrosion inhibition efficacy of 98.8% versus 84.9% for the natural inhibitor. The authors assumed that this differential was due to the synthetic inhibitor's increased concentration of active components [119–128].(Abdeen et al., 2019; Levin and Redelius, 2012; Zhang et al., 2014; Walczak et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2023; Mirarco et al., 2018; Narayanan, 2016; Khodaparast and Jessen, 2021; Ibrahim et al., 2024; Fox and Bradley, 1980).

In general, these findings indicate that natural inhibitors may be just as effective, if not more so, in preventing metal corrosion. More research is needed to fully understand the mechanics of corrosion inhibition, as the specific metal and environment under study might influence an inhibitor's efficiency.

An adsorption isotherm describes the relationship between a substance's concentration in its surrounding environment and the amount of that material adsorbed onto a surface. These isotherms provide important insights into the effectiveness of organic corrosion inhibitors in reducing corrosion (Salim et al., 2021). Several generally accepted models exist to explain the adsorptive action of an organic corrosion inhibitor. These models include the Temkin, Freundlich, and Langmuir isotherms. The Langmuir isotherm states that an inhibitor's adsorption takes place on a substrate with a finite number of adhesion sites, indicating a monolayer adsorption process.

Q =
( KL × C )
/
( 1 + KL × C )
is
the
Langmuir
isotherm
equation.
where Q, C, and KL are the adsorption capacity, solution inhibitor concentration, and mg/mg of the Langmuir constant, respectively.

Freundlich isotherm: This theory postulates that the adsorption is reliant on both the inhibitor's concentration and surface area. The following is the Freundlich isotherm equation:
lnQ = lnKf +
( 1 / n )
× lnC
where n is the Freundlich exponent, K f is the Freundlich constant (mg/g (L/mg)^(1/n)), and Q is the adsorption capacity (mg/g) and C is the inhibitor concentration in the solution (mg/L).
The Temkin isotherm states that the entropy of the adsorbent-adsorbate system and the heat of adsorption both have an impact on the adsorption process (Chahmout et al., 2023).

Q = b

Temkin

equation.
in this case, Q represents the adsorption capacity (mg/g), C the inhibitor concentration in the solution (mg/L),
A the Temkin constant (L/mg), and b the Temkin constant (mg/g).

Adsorption isotherm shape provides insight into the inhibitor adsorption mechanism and performance assessment. The inhibitor clings to the metal's surface to stop corrosion, according to the Langmuir isotherm. An understanding of the efficiency of organic corrosion inhibitors can be gained from their adsorption isotherms. In a 1 M hydrochloric acid solution, surface coverage (θ) at different concentrations is computed using the widely used Langmuir and Temkin models. It is easier to design and choose efficient corrosion inhibitors for a variety of applications when one has a solid understanding of these isotherms.

Using the DFT-Density functional theory methods and Gaussian 03 software, the inhibitor molecules' whole shape was optimized. employing the 6-1G* basis set, and Beck's three-parameter exchange functional and LeeeYangeParr non-local correlation functional (B3LYP) (Chahmout et al., 2023). It was possible to analyze the inhibitor compounds' adsorption on the metal surface by using frontier molecular orbital models (HOMO and LUMO). The ionization potential (I) is roughly equal to the negative of the highest occupied molecular orbital energy (EHOMO), and the electron affinity (A) is equal to the negative of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital energy (ELUMO), according to DFTKoopman's theorem.
I = ‐ EHOMO
A = ‐ ELUMO

The electron density distributions were evaluated using natural bond orbital (NBO) analysis (Irikura and Johnson, 2006). Understanding the electron density is necessary in order to calculate the chemical reactivity parameters. Electronegativity (χ) is one of the global reactivities along with global hardness (η) and global softness (σ). These can be computed using the following formulas:
