Chemical substances known as “organic corrosion inhibitors” help prevent metal corrosion under unfavourable environmental circumstances. Organic corrosion inhibitors function via a variety of methods (Ansari et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generally, inhibition results from one or more of the following mechanisms

ce:list-item.ce:para: Adsorption: The organic corrosion inhibitors' most commonly observed mechanism is adsorption. By adhering themselves to the metal's surface, these inhibitors create an external barrier that shields the metal from the environment's corrosive elements. The amount of inhibitor coating the metal decides the degree of inhibition. Expansion of the coating area can increase the efficiency of adsorbed inhibitors on the metal surface. These mechanisms operate to shield metal surfaces from deterioration, and their efficiency can be increased by regulating the concentration of inhibitors. Wide range of parameters can influence this process, from inhibitor concentration and surface area to the presence of other species in the solution (Mihajlović et al., 2017; Amiery et al., 2023).

Formation of a protective layer: Organic inhibitors form a protective layer on metal surfaces to prevent corrosion by using electrostatic repulsion. They prevent corrosive materials from contacting the metal surface, with positively charged inhibitors rejecting positively charged ions and negatively charged inhibitors denying negatively charged ions. This process prevents the formation of a protective layer (Amiery et al., 2021; Salman et al., 2019).
Charge on the metal surface: In this kind of mechanism, the inhibitor binds to the metal surface by means of electrostatic forces between the metal's charges and the inhibitor's ion or dipole groups.

Chemical reaction: In this process, the inhibitor's functional group and/or chemical structure have an influence. To create a coordinate bond, one electron might go from the inhibitor to the metal surface. This method is usually chosen for metals that have low-energy, unoccupied electron orbitals, such as transition metals.

Water molecules and inhibitor interaction: In this process, the inhibitors undergo a replacement reaction with the water on the metal surface, which leads to the removal of water molecules from the metal surface. A considerable portion of the free energy change on adsorption is caused by the change in the interaction energy with water during the inhibitor adsorption process, which goes from the adsorbed forms to the dissolved form. This raises the inhibitor molecules' solvation energy, which is correlated with the size of the hydrocarbon side in the inhibitor's chemical structure. As a result, when hydrocarbon size increases, solubility drops, accelerating the adsorption process.

Electrochemical reaction of inhibitors: A novel molecule exhibiting inhibitory effect is formed when some inhibitors undergo an electrochemical reduction reaction with the metal surface. The product of the electrochemical reaction is referred to as the secondary inhibitor, while the inhibitor that is put to the metal surface is known as the main inhibitor. With this kind of inhibitive mechanism, the inhibitor's effectiveness may fluctuate over time, either increasing or decreasing depending on how closely the primary inhibitor's efficacy is linked to that of the secondary inhibitor. As an example, sulfoxides (RSOR’) can be reduced to sulfides (S-2), which function better as a secondary inhibitor.

Diffusion Wall: In this mechanism, the corrosion inhibitor created a barrier on the metal's surface, physically preventing any ions or molecules from moving to or from the metal's surface, which lowers the rate of corrosion. This barrier affects the reactions that occur anodically and cathodically. This method typically operates in large inhibitor structures, such as those found in proteins, polysaccharides, or compounds with lengthy hydrocarbon chains.

Participation in the electrode processes: Adsorbed intermediate compounds with the atoms on the metal surface might occasionally be the basis of corrosion reactions. The production of these intermediates may be hampered by the inhibitors. However, if the intermediates have the inhibitors, the electrode processes may then proceed via different channels. In these stages, the inhibitors act as a catalyst and don't change. Inhibitors of corrosion may also slow down the rate at which hydrogen releases from metals by altering the mechanism of the process by raising the Tafel slopes of cathodic polarization curves.

ce:section.ce:para: The following equations depict how inhibitors coat the metal surface to keep corrosive substances from penetrating the steel. For the purpose of creating efficient corrosion inhibitors for mild steel applications, it is essential to understand this mechanism.

M + Inhi ↔ M (Inhi)ads
M (Inhi)ads ↔ M2+ + Inhi + ne-
Inhi + H2Oads ↔ Inhiads + H2O (aq)
M = Metal, Inhi = Inhibitor.

The donor-acceptor interactions between the lone pair of electrons from the inhibitors' donor groups (carbonyl and amine groups) and the d-orbitals of metal indicate the chemisorption of inhibitors on mild steel. The free energy resulting from the inhibitors' adsorption had a greater negative value. This demonstrates that the inhibitors' adsorption process on the mild steel coupon's surface was chemically adsorbed. The process of inhibiting corrosion starts with the displacement of water molecules due to the inhibitor's ability to specifically adsorb on the metal's surface. The polarization results indicate that varying the inhibitor concentration for the various polarization tests did not yield a statistically significant change in the numerical values of βa and βc. This indicates that the mechanism of inhibition of the anodic and cathodic corrosion reactions remains unchanged. The adsorption of the inhibitor molecule's conjugated π-electrons, oxygen, nitrogen, and other electro-active atoms with the negatively charged centres of the metal surface suggested a similar type of action (Ituen et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organic corrosion inhibitors function through a variety of methods (Amiery et al., 2023)
ce:section.ce:para:
Chemical substances known as “organic corrosion inhibitors” help prevent metal corrosion under unfavourable environmental circumstances. There are multiple ways in which organic corrosion inhibitors operate.
1.
pH Adjustment: The pH of the solution can also be made neutral or slightly alkaline by using organic corrosion inhibitors. By doing this, the corrosive level of chemicals in the solution is lowered, slowing down the pace of corrosion.
2.
Cathodic Protection: By lowering the rate of the cathodic reaction and, in turn, the rate of corrosion, organic corrosion inhibitors can operate as cathodic inhibitors.
3.
Oxygen Scavenging: By scavenging oxygen out of the solution, organic corrosion inhibitors can slow the rate of corrosion. Corrosive substances that might normally cause corrosion are prevented from developing by eliminating the oxygen (Erdoğan and Tüzün, 2023; Emmanuel, 2024; Kharafi et al., 1986).
4.
Computational Methods: Computational methods have gained more importance in corrosion inhibition research in the past few years. These are a few instances of the application of computational methods in corrosion inhibition research (Gurjar et al., 2021; Chaouiki et al., 2022; Sharma et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2003; Bouammali et al., 2024).
5.
Simulations of molecular dynamics (MDs): MD simulations provide information about the atomic-level interactions between inhibitors and metal surfaces. These simulations help us understand inhibitor behaviour during adsorption, the effect of molecular structure on inhibitor performance, and how environmental conditions like pH and temperature affect inhibitor efficiency.
6.
Density functional theory (DFT): The electrical characteristics and interactions of inhibitors with metal surfaces can be investigated by DFT calculations. This method can be used to determine which inhibitors are furthermost active for a given metal surface and to forecast the adsorption energy of the inhibitors.
7.
Monte Carlo simulations: rewrite. This can help anticipate the extent and distribution of inhibitors on metal surfaces. Such an approach provides insights into the mechanism that underpins inhibitor effectiveness and can help optimize inhibitor dosages and administration methods.
8.
Machine learning: These algorithms can be trained on large datasets of corrosion inhibition data to predict the efficacy of novel inhibitors. This technique speeds up the discovery of novel inhibitors while minimizing the time and cost associated with experimental screening.

Computational techniques are often very important in corrosion inhibition research because they enable researchers to gain a better understanding of the mechanisms underpinning inhibitor action as well as the creation and application of corrosion inhibitors.

Environmental factors can induce corrosion, which is a slow degradation of components that can harm oil and gas installations. It may happen in any environment containing an aqueous solution and involves an electrolyte, an anode, and a cathode in challenging pipeline system conditions (Oguntuyi et al., 2021). In order to reach the cathode, which contains the corroding metal, electrons must first travel from the anode via the electrolyte. Transporting very impure materials, such as steel and its alloys, is possible with natural gas and crude oil (Zhang et al., 2023). Unfortunately, exposure to acidic media causes these materials to corrode and deteriorate significantly. Hydrogen sulfide, carbon dioxide, and water are examples of highly corrosive media that can cause corrosion in gas and oil wells and (Villamizar et al., 2006).

As a result of well souring, fluid changes, operational temperatures and pressures, and other factors, the continual passage of H2S, CO2, and water through gas and oil materials can corrode the material's interior surface, prematurely aging lines and fittings. Material failure may result from this loss in mechanical properties, which calls for adjustments to the manufacturing assembly. These characteristics include impact strength, density, ductility, and hardness (Shukla and Quraishi, 2009). Equipment design and manufacturing are impacted by corrosion, which is a major concern for the energy industry. Both financial losses and environmental harm totalling billions of dollars have come from it. Environmental pollution and oil leaks have resulted from the corrosion-induced equipment damage suffered by the oil pipeline industry. Petroleum, mechanical, and chemical engineers have difficulties since corrosion also alters the chemistry and purity of materials (Quraishi et al., 2007; Song, 2010).

Oxidizing acids react with steel, creating an oxide layer that inhibits further corrosion. Non-oxidizing acids produce iron salts, and strong sulphuric acids produce iron salts. Techniques to protect steel include environmental changes, cathodic protection, material modification, and corrosion inhibitors. However, these methods have limitations, such as difficulty or cost, and may not be suitable for industrial practices due to specific reactive solutions. Corrosion inhibitors offer a user-friendly and less expensive solution, offering an alternative to these challenges. An inhibitor is a substance used to inhibit corrosion in the steel pipeline industry by creating a shielding film. These inhibitors prevent corrosion in in-service steel materials like heat exchangers, boilers, and gas containers. They are used in structural treatments to restrict corrosion reactions and metal deterioration. However, their toxicity is a major factor, leading to the development of alternative green methods that prioritize atomic economy, waste prevention, energy efficiency, pollution reduction, safer solvent development, and accident prevention (Mobin and Rizvi, 2017).

Green inhibitors are eco-friendly methods used in corrosion prevention, derived from plants like oils and their derivatives. These ionic liquids, also known as green solvents, are versatile chemical, physical, and biological compounds that can operate in diverse acidic solutions. They are low-cost, biodegradable, and available, making them a cost-effective and environmentally friendly alternative to traditional corrosion inhibitors. These ionic liquids are widely used in chemical engineering due to their promising features and low ecosystem pollution. Green corrosion inhibitor drugs, combining synthetic and natural components, have gained interest in recent years due to their potential to minimize environmental pollution and disposal costs. Studies have shown the effectiveness of drugs as corrosion shielding, with some studies achieving optimum inhibition of 93.89% at an inhibitor concentration of 4000 ppm in 0.5 M HCl. Surfactants and biopolymers have also been found to be effective as green corrosion shields. Ionic liquids have also shown potential as corrosion protectors, with the Cnicus benedictus extract achieving an IE of 92.45% on mild steel. The use of Vicia sativa weed as an inhibitor in 0.5 M HCl for mild steel also demonstrated its protective ability (Mobin et al., 2019a,b).

Corrosion inhibitors play an important role in preventing metals and alloys from deteriorating in a variety of sectors. Organic inhibitors have been widely employed because to their effectiveness, ease of usage, and economic viability.

The review paper “Corrosion Inhibitors: Natural and Synthetic Organic Inhibitors” (Khalid Al-Azzawi et al., 2023; Gece, 2012; Sharma et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022; Popova et al., 2003; Shashirekha et al., 2024) provides a complete analysis of both natural and synthetic organic inhibitors, including their modes of action and recent advancements in their development. With a variety of research fields ready to contribute to the development of more effective and ecologically friendly corrosion inhibitors, the field's future seems bright. Here are some possible future advancements.

Interest in creating green corrosion inhibitors is rising as people's concerns about the effects of chemical inhibitors on the environment grow. These inhibitors provide less of a threat to the environment, are biodegradable, and are derived from renewable resources. As possible green inhibitors, investigators are investigating natural sources such biopolymers, essential oils, and plant extracts.

This has demonstrated promising results in the advancement of corrosion inhibitors by improving the protective properties of coatings and films. To increase their potency, scientists are investigating the use of nanoparticles as either carriers for inhibitors or inhibitors itself.

This has emerged as a critical tool for designing and creating novel inhibitors. As computing power and molecular modeling tools improve, researchers can obtain a better understanding of the interactions between inhibitors and metal surfaces, allowing them to design inhibitor structures for maximum effectiveness.

One fascinating area of research is combining various inhibitors to produce synergistic effects. In addition to coatings and cathodic protection, researchers are looking into the use of synthetic and natural inhibitors to prevent corrosion.
To improve performance and cost-effectiveness, increased research is expected to focus on designing inhibitors for specific industries, such as oil & gas, automotive, and aerospace.

In simple terms, corrosion inhibitor development is a lively field of study with promising prospects. Scientists are researching new materials, technologies, and approaches to create inhibitors that are not only more environmentally friendly, but also customized to specific uses and extremely effective. For those involved in this field, the review paper is a useful resource.

Corrosion is a multifaceted process that can take many different forms and is affected by numerous variables. Organic corrosion inhibitors have been studied in great detail and are generally acknowledged as an effective way to stop metal from corroding. Numerous forms of corrosion and organic corrosion inhibitors were examined in this review. The active functional groups of organic inhibitors were also covered, with a focus on their function in inhibiting corrosion.

One of the most important aspects of choosing and using corrosion inhibitors is estimating the effectiveness of organic inhibitors. For this, a number of technologies have been tested, including surface analysis techniques and electrochemical techniques. Prior studies have demonstrated the efficacy of synthetic and natural organic inhibitors in deterring corrosion. However, the kind of inhibitor, the environment, and the metal all affect how effective they are. The interaction between inhibitors and metal surfaces is shown by adsorption isotherms. After a thorough analysis, the kinetics of corrosion modeling were shown to be a valuable tool for learning about the kinetics of corrosion processes and the suppression of corrosion by organic inhibitors. Lastly, an essential component in stopping metal corrosion is organic corrosion inhibitors. To boost the effectiveness of organic inhibitors and comprehend their mechanisms better, more research is required. The creation of novel, high-quality organic inhibitors will help prolong the life of metal structures and lessen the effects of corrosion on metal surfaces.

Praveen: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Bharath K. Devendra: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision.
